Sections
A–I

Full
Expansion
EN
Language
Module 1
Fundamentals of Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence
Section / Root
Subsection
Concept / Term
Advantage / Positive
Exception / Warning
Cost / Risk
Special Note
A
The AI Ecosystem
A
A. The AI Ecosystem │ ├── A1. The Four Terms │ ├── Artificial Intelligence (AI) │ │ ├── Umbrella field │ │ └── Enables human-like intelligence in machines │ ├── Machine Learning (ML) │ │ ├── Subset of AI │ │ └── Learns patterns from data — no explicit rules │ ├── Deep Learning (DL) │ │ ├── Subset of ML │ │ └── Layered neural networks — brain-inspired structure │ └── Generative AI │ ├── Subset of DL │ └── Creates new content — text, images, audio, code │ └── A2. Hierarchy ├── AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL ⊃ Generative AI ├── Nested layers — not alternatives └── Generative AI creates · Traditional ML analyzes/predicts
B
Machine Learning Fundamentals
B
B. Machine Learning Fundamentals │ ├── B1. ML Pipeline │ ├── 01 · Data Collection — gather raw data │ ├── 02 · Data Preparation — clean, preprocess, split │ ├── 03 · Training — learn patterns, adjust parameters │ ├── 04 · Evaluation — measure performance on held-out data │ ├── 05 · Iteration — improve and repeat │ └── Model quality = Data quality · "Garbage in, garbage out" │ ├── B2. Data Types │ ├── Labeled Data │ │ ├── Correct answers attached to each data point │ │ └── Required for supervised learning · Expensive at scale │ ├── Unlabeled Data │ │ ├── No annotations — raw data only │ │ └── Abundant and cheap · Used in unsupervised / self-supervised │ ├── Structured Data │ │ ├── Tabular — rows and columns (customer records, financials) │ │ └── Time-series — sequential data over time (stock prices, sensors) │ └── Unstructured Data │ ├── Text — documents, emails, posts, code │ └── Image / Video — photos, surveillance, video streams │ ├── B3. Learning Types │ ├── Supervised Learning │ │ ├── Input: labeled data │ │ ├── Tasks: classification, regression │ │ └── Most widely used in production ML │ ├── Unsupervised Learning │ │ ├── Input: unlabeled data │ │ └── Tasks: clustering, anomaly detection, dimensionality reduction │ ├── Reinforcement Learning │ │ ├── Agent learns via reward / penalty signals │ │ ├── No labeled data — learns through interaction │ │ └── AWS: DeepRacer (autonomous RL race car) │ └── Semi-Supervised Learning │ ├── Small labeled set + large unlabeled set │ └── Practical when full labeling is cost-prohibitive │ └── B4. Inference ├── Definition — using a trained model to make predictions in production ├── Batch Inference │ ├── Large dataset processed at once │ ├── Lower cost per prediction │ └── Higher latency — not real-time └── Real-Time Inference ├── Per-request, instant predictions ├── Low latency └── Higher cost per prediction
C
Deep Learning and Neural Networks
C
C. Deep Learning and Neural Networks │ ├── C1. Neural Network Architecture │ ├── Nodes (Neurons) — processing units │ ├── Connections — weighted links between nodes │ ├── Input Layer — receives raw data (one node per feature) │ ├── Hidden Layers — learn abstract representations │ │ └── "Deep" = many hidden layers │ ├── Output Layer — produces final result │ └── Learning Process │ ├── Make prediction → compute error │ ├── Backpropagation — adjust weights throughout network │ └── Repeat over thousands/millions of iterations │ └── C2. Application Domains ├── Computer Vision (CV) │ ├── Image Classification — what is in this image? │ ├── Object Detection — where are objects, what are they? │ ├── Segmentation — which pixels belong to which object? │ └── AWS: Amazon Rekognition └── Natural Language Processing (NLP) ├── Text Classification ├── Sentiment Analysis ├── Machine Translation ├── Text Generation └── AWS: Comprehend · Translate · Lex
D
Generative AI and Foundation Models
D
D. Generative AI and Foundation Models │ ├── D1. Generative AI Outputs │ ├── Text · Images · Audio · Video · Code │ └── Key enablers: compute power + large diverse datasets │ ├── D2. Foundation Models (FMs) │ ├── Pretrained on internet-scale, unlabeled data │ ├── General-purpose — one model, many tasks │ ├── Multi-task capability — write, summarize, translate, code │ ├── Starting point for new projects — no training from scratch │ └── Amazon Bedrock — managed FM access (Anthropic, Meta, Mistral, Amazon) │ └── D3. FM Lifecycle (6 stages) ├── 01 · Data Selection — large unlabeled datasets ├── 02 · Pre-training — self-supervised learning (no labels) │ └── Continuous Pre-Training — expand knowledge to new domains ├── 03 · Optimization — fine-tuning, RLHF ├── 04 · Evaluation — benchmarks and metrics ├── 05 · Deployment — production integration ├── 06 · Feedback Loop — continuous improvement └── Self-supervised = no human labels required for pretraining
E
Generative Model Architectures
E
E. Generative Model Architectures │ ├── E1. Large Language Models (LLMs) │ ├── Architecture: Transformer — dominant design for text generation │ ├── Trained on vast text data — internet, books, open sources │ ├── Tokens │ │ ├── Smallest processing unit — words, subwords, punctuation, spaces │ │ └── Standardizes input data │ ├── Embeddings │ │ ├── Numerical vector representations of tokens │ │ └── Semantically similar words → vectors close together │ │ └── e.g. "cat" · "feline" · "kitten" → nearby in vector space │ └── Vectors │ ├── Mathematical objects capturing semantic relationships │ └── Flow: Text → Tokens → Embeddings → Transformer layers → Output token │ ├── E2. Diffusion Models │ ├── Core idea: learn to reverse a noise-adding process │ ├── Forward Diffusion (training) │ │ ├── Start with clean data │ │ └── Gradually add noise → pure noise │ ├── Reverse Diffusion (inference) │ │ ├── Start with pure random noise │ │ ├── Iteratively remove noise (guided by prompt/condition) │ │ └── Produces coherent generated image or audio │ └── Leading architecture for image/audio generation │ ├── E3. Multimodal Models │ ├── Process multiple data types simultaneously │ ├── Combinations: Text + Image · Image + Audio · Text → Image │ └── Use cases: VQA · video captioning · image generation from text │ ├── E4. GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks) │ ├── Two competing neural networks │ ├── Generator │ │ ├── Creates synthetic / fake data from noise │ │ └── Goal: produce fakes indistinguishable from real data │ ├── Discriminator │ │ ├── Receives real data + Generator's fakes │ │ └── Goal: correctly classify real vs. fake │ ├── Training: adversarial competition improves both networks │ └── Largely superseded by Diffusion models (training instability) │ └── E5. VAEs (Variational Autoencoders) ├── Encoder │ ├── Compresses input to lower-dimensional space │ └── Outputs distribution parameters (mean + variance) ├── Decoder │ ├── Samples from the distribution │ └── Reconstructs / generates data from sample ├── Latent space follows Gaussian (Normal) distribution └── Predictable, controllable generation — known probability space
F
Model Optimization Techniques
F
F. Model Optimization Techniques │ ├── F1. Prompt Engineering │ ├── Design better model inputs — no model changes │ ├── Instruction — define the task │ ├── Context — background info to guide interpretation │ ├── Input Data — raw content to act on │ ├── Output Indicator — specify format or constraints │ ├── Fastest and cheapest optimization — zero compute cost │ └── No weight modification │ ├── F2. Fine-Tuning │ ├── Continue training a pretrained FM on smaller domain-specific data │ ├── Modifies model weights │ ├── Instruction Tuning │ │ └── Train on (prompt → ideal response) pairs │ └── RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) │ ├── Humans rate / compare model responses │ ├── Reward model trained on human preferences │ └── FM fine-tuned to maximize reward score │ └── F3. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) ├── Retrieve external documents at inference time ├── Inject retrieved context into the prompt ├── Model generates response grounded in retrieved knowledge ├── No weight modification — operates at inference time only ├── Access to private, current, or domain-specific data ├── RAG ≠ Fine-Tuning — RAG changes inputs, not weights └── Cost hierarchy ├── Prompt Engineering — cheapest / fastest ├── RAG — moderate └── Fine-Tuning — most expensive / deepest change
G
AWS AI/ML Infrastructure
G
G. AWS AI/ML Infrastructure │ ├── G1. ML Framework Layer │ └── Amazon SageMaker │ ├── Build — notebooks, data prep, feature store │ ├── Train — distributed training on managed compute │ └── Deploy — real-time endpoints, batch transform, serverless │ ├── G2. AI/ML Services Layer │ ├── Amazon Comprehend — NLP: entities, sentiment, key phrases, topics │ ├── Amazon Translate — neural machine translation, 75+ language pairs │ ├── Amazon Textract — OCR: extract text, tables, forms from documents │ ├── Amazon Lex — chatbots/voice (ASR + NLU · understands intent) │ ├── Amazon Polly — text-to-speech (TTS · voiceovers, accessibility) │ ├── Amazon Transcribe — speech-to-text only (ASR) │ ├── Amazon Rekognition — image/video: objects, faces, content moderation │ ├── Amazon Kendra — intelligent enterprise search (NL queries) │ ├── Amazon Personalize — real-time personalized recommendations │ └── AWS DeepRacer — reinforcement learning experimentation platform │ └── G3. Generative AI Layer ├── Amazon Bedrock — managed FM access (Anthropic · Meta · Mistral · Amazon) ├── SageMaker JumpStart — prebuilt ML solutions · one-click deployable models ├── Amazon Q — AI business assistant · answers from internal data └── Amazon Q Developer — AI code generation, review, debugging
H
Advantages of AWS AI Solutions
H
H. Advantages of AWS AI Solutions │ └── H1. Why Build AI on AWS ├── Faster Development — prebuilt APIs · ship in days, not months ├── Scalability — global infrastructure · auto-scale inference ├── Cost Efficiency — pay-as-you-go · no upfront GPU investment ├── Flexibility — open-source + proprietary FMs via Bedrock and SageMaker ├── Integration — native S3 · Lambda · API Gateway · DynamoDB connectivity ├── Security — IAM · VPC isolation · encryption · compliance certs └── Key exam framing: AWS = rapid AI deployment without heavy infrastructure
I
Cost Considerations
I
I. Cost Considerations in AWS AI │ └── I1. Cost-Performance Trade-offs ├── Responsiveness │ └── Lower latency → higher compute cost ├── Availability │ └── Higher uptime → more redundancy cost ├── Redundancy │ └── Backup systems prevent outages → add cost ├── Performance │ └── Larger models → higher cost per inference ├── Pricing Models │ ├── Token-based Pricing │ │ ├── Pay per input/output token consumed │ │ └── Default Bedrock billing · scales with usage │ ├── Provisioned Throughput │ │ ├── Reserve dedicated model capacity upfront │ │ ├── Higher fixed cost │ │ └── Lower per-request cost at high volume │ └── Custom Models │ ├── Training compute cost │ └── Dedicated hosting cost — highest tier └── Balance cost, performance, and scalability — no single-dimension optimization
Module Summary
  • AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL ⊃ Generative AI — strict nesting. Generative AI creates; traditional ML analyzes or predicts.
  • ML pipeline: Collect → Prepare → Train → Evaluate → Iterate. Quality is data quality. Inference = prediction in production (batch vs. real-time).
  • Neural networks: input → hidden → output layers. FMs pretrain via self-supervised learning on internet-scale unlabeled data across 6 lifecycle stages.
  • Five architectures: LLMs (Transformer + tokens/embeddings/vectors) · Diffusion (forward/reverse noise) · Multimodal · GANs (Generator vs. Discriminator) · VAEs (Encoder → Gaussian distribution → Decoder).
  • Optimization: Prompt Engineering (no weight change) → RAG (external knowledge, no weights) → Fine-Tuning (modifies weights). AWS stack: SageMaker · 10 managed AI APIs · Bedrock / Q / JumpStart.
AWS-AI MasterClass · Module 01 · Fundamentals of Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence
